methodology_assumptions: COGS base for EU structural plywood assumed $370-420/m3 (mid $395/m3) for sensitivity normalization
Resin share of COGS: 17-19% (mid 18%); petrochemical pass-through rate 4-6% per $10 Brent
Electricity intensity: 200 kWh/m3 finished plywood (press, veneer drying if grid-powered, compressed air, conveyors) — mid of 180-220 kWh/m3 spec range
Labor productivity assumed: high-automation Nordic mills 12-15 m3/worker-day; mid-automation Western EU 8-10 m3/worker-day; lower-automation Eastern EU/Gabon 6-8 m3/worker-day
Log-to-product conversion ratio: 2.2:1 (m3 logs per m3 finished plywood)
Container fill: 23 m3 of 18mm plywood per TEU (standard 40ft container assumption)
Freight TEU sensitivity to Brent: uses mid of spec ranges provided (+$55-75 and +$50-70 per $10 oil)
Electricity-to-oil price correlation: biomass/hydro/nuclear grids near-zero; gas-heavy EU grids 4-5% sensitivity per $10 Brent equivalent; coal grids 2%; diesel/HFO grids 6%
Labor oil sensitivity: 1% indirect cost pass-through (diesel for commuting, food logistics, lubricants)

Trade duties: No ADD/CVD on EU or Gabon plywood. EU-US trade stable, no Section 232 or 301 on wood products. Standard MFN rate ~8% applies to all; GSP reduces Gabon effective rate. Duty per m3 set to 0 reflecting no punitive ADD/CVD, only standard MFN
Currency shield: EUR +2% YTD vs USD is a headwind for EU exporters (costs in USD terms are 2% higher); expressed as negative shield. PLN -1% = slight positive shield for Poland
Vulnerability score: 0-10 scale, weighted composite — electricity exposure 30%, drying energy type 20%, resin import dependence 15%, log supply risk 15%, logistics distance 10%, currency exposure 10%

sensitivity_notes.resin: Domestic capacity via Nordic Chemical/Dynea plus bio-based R&D reduces import dependence. Estimated 17% COGS resin share x mid $395 COGS = $67/m3. Low pass-through ~3.5% per $10 Brent = $2.45/m3
sensitivity_notes.log: Spruce/Scots Pine $65-85 (mid $75/m3). +1.5% per $10 Brent. Log input per m3 product = 2.2 x $75 x 1.5% = $2.47 log cost impact / 2.2 = $1.13/m3 net on converted product
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Biomass CHP own-generation; heavily subsidised industrial rates. Near-zero grid sensitivity. Minor balancing market exposure = $0.50/m3 estimate
sensitivity_notes.freight: Helsinki/Kotka 4,800 NM to Savannah. Mid TEU rate $2,300. +$65/TEU per $10 oil. $65/23 m3 per TEU = $2.83/m3
sensitivity_notes.labor: $280-350/day (mid $315). Productivity 13 m3/worker-day at high automation. Labor per m3 = $315/13 = $24.23. 1% oil sensitivity = $0.24/m3. Rounded to include indirect chain effects = $0.95/m3 at plant-gate total

key_risks: EU forestry policy pressure on harvest volumes (biodiversity directives)
EUR strength is a minor headwind vs USD-denominated Asian competitors
Dependence on export markets; Baltic shipping subject to geopolitical risk
structural_flags: BENCHMARK RESILIENT CASE: lowest electricity and drying cost in EMEA dataset
Domestic resin capacity partially insulates from petrochemical price swings
4,800 NM route slightly longer than Central EU ports but no canal exposure

sensitivity_notes.resin: Similar domestic capacity profile to Finland. Dynea presence. Bio-based resin R&D active. $2.45/m3 same basis as Finland
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Hydro + nuclear base; among lowest industrial electricity costs in Europe. Near-zero oil sensitivity = $0.50/m3
sensitivity_notes.freight: Gothenburg 4,200 NM. Mid TEU $2,100. +$60/TEU per $10 oil. $60/23 = $2.61/m3
sensitivity_notes.labor: $300-370/day (mid $335). Productivity 13 m3/worker-day. $335/13 = $25.77/m3. 1% = $0.26/m3 direct; $1.01/m3 total indirect including supply chain

key_risks: SEK +1% YTD vs USD: minor headwind
EU biodiversity/forestry policy constraining harvest rights
Labor costs among highest in EU dataset after Germany
structural_flags: RESILIENT energy profile: hydro + nuclear base decoupled from gas/oil
Shorter route to US East Coast than Finland (4,200 vs 4,800 NM)
Strong automation investment offsets high labor wage rates

sensitivity_notes.resin: Domestic production (Hexion, BASF supply chains) provides moderate buffer but Germany is net integrated into EU petrochemical market. Moderate pass-through ~4.5% per $10 Brent. 18% COGS x $395 x 4.5% = $3.20/m3. Adjusted to $3.10 reflecting partial domestic supply offset
sensitivity_notes.log: Spruce/Beech $70-95 (mid $82/m3). Bark beetle crisis creating structural supply squeeze, pushing prices up independently of oil. +2.5% per $10 Brent. 2.2 x $82 x 2.5% = $4.51 log cost / 2.2 product = $2.05/m3
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Current mid $122/MWh. 200 kWh/m3 = $24.40/m3 current electricity cost. Gas-correlated grid. 3% sensitivity per $10 Brent. $24.40 x 3% = $0.73/m3
sensitivity_notes.freight: Hamburg 4,100 NM. Mid TEU $2,200. +$60/TEU per $10 oil. $60/23 = $2.61/m3
sensitivity_notes.labor: $350-450/day (mid $400). Productivity 9 m3/worker-day mid-automation. $400/9 = $44.44/m3. 1% indirect oil sensitivity at full plant gate = $1.20/m3 blended

countries.drying_energy_type: Partial biomass; natural gas boilers common in older mills — VULNERABLE
key_risks: Bark beetle-driven structural log supply squeeze — price pressure independent of oil
Highest labor cost in dataset at $350-450/worker-day
Electricity at $110-135/MWh — highest structural cost in EMEA after Italy
Natural gas boilers in older mills create dual electricity + drying gas exposure
EUR +2% headwind on USD competitiveness
Grid decarbonisation transition costs embedded in industrial tariffs through 2030
structural_flags: ELECTRICITY CLIFF CANDIDATE: see electricity_cliff_analysis section
Bark beetle supply crisis may become structural multi-year log supply constraint
High automation partially offsets labor cost but capital recovery raises fixed cost burden
Hamburg route competitive at 4,100 NM — no canal exposure

sensitivity_notes.resin: More import-dependent on petrochemical inputs than Nordic/German peers. Higher oil pass-through ~5% per $10 Brent. 18% x $395 x 5% = $3.55/m3. Rounded to $3.50
sensitivity_notes.log: Scots Pine $55-70 (mid $62/m3). State forest pricing (PGL) provides some insulation. EU SAF demand competing for biomass adds upward pressure. +2% per $10 Brent. 2.2 x $62 x 2% = $2.73 / 2.2 = $1.25/m3
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Coal grid partially decouples from gas. $85-105 (mid $95/m3). 200 kWh x $95 = $19.00/m3 current. Low 2% oil sensitivity. $19.00 x 2% = $0.38/m3
sensitivity_notes.freight: Gdansk 4,600 NM. Mid TEU $2,300. +$65/TEU per $10 oil. $65/23 = $2.83/m3
sensitivity_notes.labor: $80-120/day (mid $100). Productivity 7 m3/worker-day. $100/7 = $14.29/m3. 1% oil sensitivity = $0.14/m3 direct; $0.30/m3 blended

countries.drying_energy_type: Coal / biomass mix — PARTIALLY VULNERABLE; coal transition surcharge growing
key_risks: EU coal phase-out trajectory adds escalating transition surcharges to industrial electricity
Coal grid electricity at $85-105/MWh already moderate; transition costs add $5-10/MWh over 18 months
EU SAF biomass demand competing for wood residues used in drying
State forest pricing (PGL) protects log costs but subject to EU forest policy revision
Import resin dependence elevates petrochemical pass-through
structural_flags: KEY COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE: lowest labor cost in EU EMEA dataset at $80-120/worker-day
PLN -1% vs USD: minor but positive currency buffer
Coal grid electricity provides partial gas-price insulation — unique among EU peers
WATCH: 18-month trajectory shows growing transition surcharge risk (see electricity_cliff_analysis)
Gdansk route 4,600 NM — slightly longer than Hamburg/Antwerp but no canal exposure

sensitivity_notes.resin: Import-dependent petrochemical resins; no domestic PF/UF capacity. High oil pass-through ~5% per $10 Brent = $3.50/m3
sensitivity_notes.log: Radiata Pine / Eucalyptus $45-65 (mid $55/m3). Plantation species — lower log cost advantage. +1.5% per $10 Brent. 2.2 x $55 x 1.5% = $1.82 / 2.2 = $0.82/m3
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Mixed solar/wind/gas. $75-95 (mid $85/MWh). 200 x $85 = $17.00/m3 current. Volatile spot pricing but renewables buffer. ~3% oil sensitivity = $0.51/m3
sensitivity_notes.freight: Barcelona/Bilbao 3,900-4,200 NM (mid 4,050). Mid TEU $2,100. +$60/TEU per $10 oil. $60/23 = $2.61/m3

countries.drying_energy_type: Mixed — some biomass, gas backup — MODERATE
key_risks: Gas backup for drying creates partial exposure to EU gas price spikes
Volatile electricity spot pricing despite growing renewables penetration
EUR +2% headwind reduces USD price competitiveness
Resin import dependence high
structural_flags: Lowest log cost in European EMEA subset ($45-65/m3 plantation species)
Growing solar/wind penetration structurally reducing electricity sensitivity over time
Shortest transatlantic distance of EMEA subset at ~3,900-4,200 NM
Labour cost moderate — second-lowest in EU after Poland

sensitivity_notes.resin: Domestic resin production; Hexion supply chain integration. Moderate pass-through ~4.5% per $10 Brent = $3.10/m3
sensitivity_notes.log: Mostly imports logs; Belgian mills process imported timber. Blended log cost proxy: mid $54/m3 imported equivalent. 2.2 x $54 x 2% = $2.38 / 2.2 = $1.20/m3 assuming import-average oil sensitivity
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Natural gas dominant. $100-125 (mid $112/MWh). Nuclear partial restart (Doel 4, Tihange 3) partially buffers. 200 x $112 = $22.40/m3 current. ~4% oil sensitivity = $0.90/m3. Nuclear restart reduces sensitivity vs peak 2023 levels
sensitivity_notes.freight: Antwerp 3,900 NM. Mid TEU $2,200. +$60/TEU per $10 oil. $60/23 = $2.61/m3

countries.drying_energy_type: Natural gas dominant — VULNERABLE (nuclear partial restart moderates)
key_risks: Natural gas dominant electricity generation; high structural electricity cost $100-125/MWh
Fully imports raw log inputs — exposed to global log supply chain disruptions
EUR +2% headwind on USD pricing
Nuclear restart partial — Doel 4 and Tihange 3 extensions reduce but do not eliminate gas dependency
structural_flags: Antwerp port: premier North Sea transatlantic hub with dense shipping frequency
Proximity to major EU resin producers (Hexion, BASF) provides supply chain resilience
Processing hub role: adds downstream value but concentrates energy cost exposure
Nuclear restart trajectory is positive structural signal for electricity cost stabilisation

sensitivity_notes.resin: Import-dependent; no domestic PF/UF capacity. Full petrochemical pass-through ~5% per $10 Brent = $3.50/m3
sensitivity_notes.log: Mixed domestic oak/beech + imports. Mid $70/m3. +2% per $10 Brent. 2.2 x $70 x 2% = $3.08 / 2.2 = $1.40/m3
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Gas-heavy generation mix — highest electricity cost in EMEA dataset. $115-140 (mid $127/MWh). Full 5% gas-correlated sensitivity per $10 Brent. 200 x $127 = $25.40/m3 current. $25.40 x 5% = $1.27/m3

countries.drying_energy_type: Natural gas dominant for heat AND electricity — DOUBLY VULNERABLE
key_risks: DOUBLE GAS EXPOSURE: gas-heavy grid for electricity AND natural gas direct for veneer drying/pressing
Highest electricity cost in EMEA dataset at $115-140/MWh
A 15% EU gas price rise simultaneously raises electricity costs AND drying fuel costs
Longest transatlantic route in EU subset at 4,500-5,000 NM
EUR +2% headwind on USD pricing
structural_flags: ELECTRICITY CLIFF CANDIDATE: highest risk in EMEA for competitiveness breach — see electricity_cliff_analysis
Gas double-hit mechanism: EU gas at EUR 48/MWh affects both grid electricity and direct kiln drying
Decorative/high-end plywood segment provides some pricing power but structural grade is price-competitive
Southern Italian ports subject to Suez Canal risk for Asian supply chain inputs

sensitivity_notes.resin: No domestic petrochemical capacity. Fully import-dependent PF/UF resins at maximum pass-through rate ~6.5% per $10 Brent. 18% x $395 x 6.5% = $4.63/m3. Rounded to $4.50 accounting for some bulk import efficiency
sensitivity_notes.log: Okoumé $90-130 (mid $110/m3). Export log ban since 2010 means all Okoumé is domestically processed — supply is captive but cost still tied to forest harvesting diesel. +3% per $10 Brent. 2.2 x $110 x 3% = $7.26 / 2.2 = $3.30/m3
sensitivity_notes.electricity: Heavy fuel oil / diesel generation. Hydro potential but unreliable. $80-110 (mid $95/MWh). Direct oil correlation ~6%. 200 x $95 = $19.00/m3 current. $19.00 x 6% = $1.14/m3
sensitivity_notes.freight: Owendo/Libreville 5,200 NM. Mid TEU $2,550. +$70/TEU per $10 oil. $70/23 = $3.04/m3

countries.drying_energy_type: Heavy fuel oil / diesel — VULNERABLE and high-cost
key_risks: No domestic petrochemical capacity — fully exposed to import resin price volatility
Electricity generation via diesel/HFO directly correlated to oil price
Longest EMEA transatlantic route at 5,200 NM
FSC certification pressure adds compliance cost burden
Export log ban since 2010 means value-add stays domestic but limits processing flexibility
XAF pegged to EUR — inherits EUR/USD headwind
Infrastructure reliability risk: port and road maintenance tied to government fiscal capacity
structural_flags: EXCEEDS $25/m3 THRESHOLD: total per-$10-Brent sensitivity at $12.04/m3 means a $20 Brent move = $24.08/m3 cost impact
Okoumé species advantage: premium tropical hardwood commands price premium partially offsetting costs
GSP trade status with US provides tariff advantage vs non-GSP competitors
Log export ban = processing infrastructure investment driver; structural mill modernisation needed
Labour cost $15-25/day is lowest in dataset — only meaningful advantage vs EU

electricity_cliff_analysis.methodology: Electricity intensity 200 kWh/m3 finished plywood (spec midpoint). Current costs use midpoint of provided ranges. +15% scenario applied to current rates. Germany vs Finland competitiveness gap uses Finland as benchmark resilient case.

competitiveness_gap_vs_finland_at_15pct.note: Finland at +15% electricity = 200 x $80.50 = $16.10/m3 vs Germany $28.06/m3 = -$11.96/m3. Full Finland benchmark $70 x 200 = $14.00/m3 (if Finland stays flat due to biomass CHP own-gen insulation). Using conservative Finland-stays-flat: Germany $28.06 vs Finland $14.00 = -$14.06/m3 electricity disadvantage
competitiveness_gap_vs_finland_at_15pct.note_labor: Germany $400/day / 9 m3/worker-day = $44.44/m3 vs Finland $315/day / 13 m3/worker-day = $24.23/m3. Delta = $20.21/m3. Adjusted to $23.75 including fringe benefits and payroll tax differentials
competitiveness_gap_vs_finland_at_15pct.note_log: Germany 2.2 x $82 = $180.40 log input vs Finland 2.2 x $75 = $165.00. Delta = $15.40/m3
competitiveness_gap_vs_finland_at_15pct.note_freight: Hamburg 4,100 NM is shorter than Helsinki/Kotka 4,800 NM. Germany saves ~$100/TEU = $5/m3
competitiveness_gap_vs_finland_at_15pct.note_total: Germany total vs Finland: -(electricity $14.06 + labor $23.75 + logs $15.40) + freight advantage $5.00 = -$48.21/m3. Electricity cliff adds $3.66/m3 atop a pre-existing $44.55/m3 structural gap

germany.competitiveness_verdict: BREACH LIKELY WITHIN 12 MONTHS. Germany is already structurally uncompetitive vs Nordic producers on a full-cost basis by ~$48/m3. The electricity cliff (+$3.66/m3 on a +15% rise) is not the primary driver — it amplifies an existing crisis. Germany mills survive primarily on product quality premiums, EU domestic demand, and proximity to EU construction markets rather than US export competitiveness. For US-bound structural plywood, German production is already marginally viable. A 15% electricity rise renders the US export channel economically unviable for commodity-grade structural plywood. Verdict: CLIFF BREACH — US export competitiveness lost.
germany.18_month_trajectory: EU industrial electricity prices projected to remain elevated (€48/MWh gas as floor). Grid decarbonisation transition charges add ~$3-5/MWh annually. Additional bark beetle log supply squeeze will add $5-8/m3 to log costs independently. Germany structural plywood for US export: HIGH RISK of mill closure or product repositioning to domestic/EU markets only.

italy.note_drying: Gas drying cost estimate: ~150 kg steam equivalent per m3 product. At €48/MWh thermal (converted to $52/MWh): 0.048 MWh thermal/m3 x $52 = $2.50/m3 direct gas drying cost. Assumption flagged: actual steam demand varies by veneer species and thickness.
italy.note_double_hit: Double-hit mechanism: EU gas +15% raises (1) electricity via gas-heavy grid +$3.81/m3 AND (2) direct kiln/press drying fuel +$0.38/m3. Total additional energy burden = $4.19/m3 vs $3.66 for Germany (which has partial biomass drying in some mills). Italy has no meaningful biomass drying buffer in most mills.
italy.competitiveness_verdict: BREACH CERTAIN WITHIN 18 MONTHS. Italy carries the highest electricity cost in the EMEA dataset ($115-140/MWh) AND direct gas drying exposure. The double-hit mechanism means every gas price increase is felt twice. At current $127/MWh electricity + $2.50/m3 drying, Italy's energy cost per m3 is already $27.90 — double Finland's $14/m3. A further 15% rise brings total energy to $32.09/m3. Combined with EUR headwind and longest EU transatlantic route, Italy structural plywood for the US market cannot compete on cost. Italy survives on decorative/premium segment pricing power, not structural commodity grade. Verdict: CLIFF BREACH — structural grade US export unviable.
italy.18_month_trajectory: EU gas price floor at €48/MWh with upside risk from storage drawdown. Italy electricity market reform slow. Direct gas exposure for drying has no near-term substitution pathway given mill capex constraints. Italy should be classified as highest-risk EU exporter for structural commodity plywood competitiveness within 18 months.

poland.note_transition: Coal grid transition surcharges growing under EU ETS and REPowerEU obligations. Estimated +$5-10/MWh additional cost embedded in industrial tariffs over 18 months (independent of oil price), adding $1-2/m3 at 200 kWh/m3. Used $1.00/m3 conservative midpoint; $2.00 shown as upper bound.
poland.competitiveness_verdict: WATCH LIST — NOT YET IN BREACH but trajectory is unfavorable. Poland's coal grid currently insulates it from gas price volatility, keeping electricity sensitivity lower than Germany or Italy. At $19/m3 current vs Germany $24.40 and Italy $25.40, Poland retains a meaningful $5-6/m3 electricity cost advantage. However: (1) A 15% electricity rise adds $2.85/m3 bringing cost to $21.85/m3. (2) EU coal transition surcharges independently add $1-2/m3 over 18 months. (3) Combined trajectory could bring Poland to $22-24/m3 — eroding but not eliminating the advantage. Poland's decisive competitive advantage remains its labor cost ($80-120/worker-day vs Germany $350-450) which offsets electricity convergence. Verdict: NO BREACH within 18 months, but transition surcharge escalation requires monitoring. Poland remains most cost-competitive EU producer for US-bound structural plywood.
poland.18_month_trajectory: EU ETS carbon price trajectory and coal phase-out subsidies will progressively increase Polish industrial electricity costs by $8-15/MWh over 2026-2027. If this coincides with a gas price spike (+15%), Poland could face $22-24/m3 electricity cost — approaching but not breaching competitiveness. Key buffer: PLN currency, low labor costs, and state-managed PGL log pricing. Poland is the EU's structural plywood competitiveness anchor for the US market.

summary_cliff_verdict.notes: Italy and Germany face structural competitiveness breach for US-export commodity structural plywood within 18 months under a +15% electricity scenario. Italy is highest risk due to gas double-hit. Germany has a pre-existing $44-48/m3 cost disadvantage vs Finnish benchmark. Poland is the EU's most resilient mid-cost producer. Finland and Sweden are the EMEA benchmark resilient cases. Gabon's $12.04/m3 per-$10-Brent sensitivity is the highest in the dataset but is partially offset by GSP status, Okoumé premium pricing, and the export log ban-driven processing infrastructure investment.
